text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer (mCRPC) — BRCA2 Germline / Somatic Biallelic Loss
Olaparib (PARP Inhibitor) Post-Transfusion Assessment / Dose Re-escalation Planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer harboring germline and somatic BRCA2 biallelic loss, presenting today for follow-up approximately 8 months into his olaparib (Lynparza) course. He is being seen following hospitalization-level management of Grade 3 anemia and associated transfusion, with today's visit focused on reassessment of blood counts, symptom burden, disease status, and safe re-initiation of olaparib at a further reduced dose.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 3 years ago with Stage IVB prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, with bone metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala, as well as pelvic lymph node involvement (right external iliac). PSA at diagnosis was 187.4 ng/mL. He was started on androgen deprivation therapy with leuprolide (Lupron Depot 22.5 mg IM q3 months) combined with abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily plus prednisone 5 mg BID, achieving an excellent initial PSA response to nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at roughly 12 months.

Unfortunately, PSA began rising at approximately 18 months and biochemical CRPC was confirmed at the 24-month mark, with PSA 8.6 ng/mL on castrate testosterone. At that time, somatic tumor profiling (CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis) confirmed BRCA2 biallelic loss (consistent with germline BRCA2 c.5946delT frameshift variant plus somatic second-hit deletion), along with AR amplification (~8x), TP53 p.R175H, and RB1 homozygous deletion. He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily, which yielded a modest PSA response (nadir 1.9 ng/mL) but proved short-lived; radiographic progression with new bone lesions (right femoral neck, T11, left acetabulum) and new PSMA-avid pelvic lymph nodes was documented at approximately 7 months. Denosumab (Prolia/Xgeva 120 mg SQ q4 weeks) was started alongside bone-protective supplementation at that time.

Olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID was initiated approximately 8 months ago given the confirmed BRCA2 biallelic somatic loss (PROfound trial FDA-approved indication). PSA at olaparib start was 18.2 ng/mL. By 3 months, PSA had declined to 5.7 ng/mL (69% reduction) with substantial symptomatic improvement in bone pain. Imaging at 6 months demonstrated partial bone scan response with decreased tracer uptake in right femoral neck, T11, and left acetabulum lesions, and right pelvic lymph nodes shrank from 0.8 cm to 0.4 cm. PSA at 6 months was 3.9 ng/mL.

However, beginning at approximately 4 months on olaparib, Mr. Kowalski developed progressive anemia: hemoglobin dropped from a pre-treatment baseline of 12.4 g/dL to 9.1 g/dL (Grade 2) at 4 months, prompting dose reduction from 300 mg BID to 200 mg BID. At 7 months, hemoglobin deteriorated further to 7.4 g/dL (Grade 3), accompanied by Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelets 128 K/uL), marked symptomatic fatigue, exertional dyspnea, and lightheadedness. He received 2 units of packed red blood cells (pRBC) in the infusion center, with post-transfusion hemoglobin recovering to 9.8 g/dL. Olaparib was held at that time. Hematology was consulted; bone marrow biopsy was deferred given the clear clinical context of PARP inhibitor-related myelosuppression. A plan was formulated to restart olaparib at a further reduced dose of 150 mg BID once hemoglobin stabilized above 9 g/dL.

Today, Mr. Kowalski presents approximately 3-4 weeks after that transfusion and olaparib hold. He reports his energy has improved considerably compared to the time of his transfusion, though he remains more fatigued than his pre-olaparib baseline. He denies active dyspnea at rest and notes he can walk short distances without significant breathlessness. Bone pain remains relatively well controlled at 1-2/10, which he attributes to the ongoing disease response. He has not had fevers, chills, or signs of infection. No new bleeding, bruising beyond mild, or neurological symptoms. He reports no chest pain or palpitations. He continues calcium 500 mg and vitamin D 400 IU daily with his denosumab.

Social context: Retired ironworker, lives with his wife. His daughter, who was informed of the germline BRCA2 finding, has completed cascade testing and was found to carry the same variant; she is being followed at an outside high-risk cancer program. Mr. Kowalski's wife accompanies him today and remains closely involved in his care.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue Grade 1-2 (improved since transfusion). No fever. Mild unintentional weight fluctuation (weight appears stable today vs. last visit). Appetite fair to good.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No palpitations. No edema. Mild exertional dyspnea with climbing stairs, markedly improved compared to pre-transfusion.
Respiratory: No cough, no hemoptysis, no pleuritic pain.
Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea intermittently; taking ondansetron PRN with good relief. No vomiting. Bowel movements regular. No hematochezia or melena.
Genitourinary: Urinary stream remains slow (pre-existing, BPH history); no hematuria. Leuprolide injection well tolerated; hot flashes persistent (Grade 1, pre-existing).
Musculoskeletal: Lumbar back pain 1-2/10 (stable, improved from prior). Right hip area pain minimal. No new joint pain or swelling.
Neurological: No seizures. No focal neurological deficits. No headaches or visual changes.
Dermatologic: Mild alopecia from prior enzalutamide/hormone therapy context (pre-existing). No skin rash.
Hematologic: Denies spontaneous bruising or bleeding. No petechiae noted by patient.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Persistent hot flashes (castration-related, managed conservatively).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Alert, oriented, in no acute distress. Appears mildly fatigued but conversant and engaged. Well-groomed. Accompanying wife.
Vital Signs: BP 126/78 mmHg, HR 84 bpm (regular), RR 16/min, SpO2 97% on room air, Temperature 36.8°C, Weight 74.2 kg.
Head and Neck: Conjunctival pallor mild. Oropharynx clear. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No thyromegaly.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1/S2 present, no murmurs. No JVD. No peripheral edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rhonchi, or rales.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No RUQ tenderness. Bowel sounds active.
Genitourinary: Deferred today (no new urologic symptoms).
Musculoskeletal: Mild lumbar paraspinal tenderness on palpation consistent with known metastatic disease at L3/L4. No new focal bony tenderness elicited at femur, hips, or ribs today. Gait slightly cautious but stable.
Extremities: No significant edema. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. No cyanosis.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves grossly intact. No focal motor deficits. Sensation intact to light touch distally (though patient endorses mild numbness in bilateral feet — unchanged from prior, pre-existing low-grade peripheral neuropathy, likely from ADT-era changes and prior disease burden rather than current chemotherapy at this time).
Skin: No rash, petechiae, or ecchymoses.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count (today):
  - Hemoglobin: 9.6 g/dL (improved from nadir of 7.4 g/dL pre-transfusion; post-transfusion recovery)
  - Hematocrit: 28.8%
  - MCV: 96 fL (mild macrocytosis, improved from prior 98 fL)
  - WBC: 4.8 K/uL (within normal limits; ANC approximately 2.9 K/uL)
  - Platelets: 112 K/uL (Grade 1 thrombocytopenia, recovering from nadir of 128 K/uL — trending toward prior 128 and not acutely worsening)
  - Reticulocyte count: 1.1% (appropriately rising vs. 0.3% at time of anemia episode — bone marrow beginning to recover)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - BUN: 22 mg/dL; Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR ~52 mL/min, stable chronic kidney disease Stage IIIa — unchanged baseline)
  - Sodium 138, Potassium 4.1, Chloride 103, Bicarbonate 24 — all within normal limits
  - Total Bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
  - ALT: 32 U/L (normal); AST: 36 U/L (normal)
  - Alkaline Phosphatase: 148 U/L (mildly elevated — known from osseous metastatic disease, baseline for this patient)
  - Albumin: 3.4 g/dL (mildly low, nutritional/systemic inflammatory effect)

PSA: 4.4 ng/mL (slight rise from nadir of approximately 3.9 ng/mL at 6-month scan; this modest elevation is in the context of approximately 3-4 weeks of olaparib hold following the Grade 3 anemia event, and is not interpreted as definitive disease progression at this time — PSA kinetics on PARP inhibitor therapy can fluctuate with dose interruptions)

Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (castrate, confirmed)

LDH: 214 U/L (mildly elevated, unchanged from prior)

Iron studies (repeat today to reassess in context of darbepoetin planning):
  - Serum Iron: 74 mcg/dL
  - Ferritin: 320 ng/mL
  - TIBC: 260 mcg/dL (normal)
  - Transferrin saturation: ~28% — no iron deficiency identified; iron stores adequate

Vitamin B12: 412 pg/mL (normal); Folate: 12 ng/mL (normal, folic acid supplementation ongoing)

---

IMAGING

Most recent imaging: Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed at 6 months on olaparib demonstrated partial response in bone metastases (decreased tracer uptake at right femoral neck, T11, left acetabulum; stable sclerotic changes at L3, L4, right iliac crest); no visceral metastases; right pelvic lymph nodes decreased to 0.4 cm. This imaging preceded the anemia events and dose reductions. No new imaging today; next restaging scan is planned for the upcoming cycle if dose re-escalation proceeds.

---

ASSESSMENT

1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), BRCA2 biallelic loss (germline frameshift variant + somatic second-hit deletion), with prior bone and lymph node metastases, currently demonstrating partial radiographic and PSA response to olaparib (Lynparza). Disease status: partial response, though PSA has modestly ticked up to 4.4 ng/mL in the context of a ~3-4 week olaparib hold — this is not yet interpreted as progression and requires close monitoring.

2. Olaparib-related Grade 3 anemia — now improving post-transfusion and olaparib hold. Hemoglobin today is 9.6 g/dL, which is above the threshold of 9 g/dL we established for safe re-initiation. Reticulocyte count is rebounding, suggesting endogenous erythropoietic recovery is beginning. The myelosuppressive pattern here — macrocytic anemia with low reticulocyte count, normal B12/folate, no iron deficiency — is entirely consistent with PARP inhibitor-related bone marrow suppression; no alternative etiology has been identified.

3. Grade 1 thrombocytopenia (platelets 112 K/uL) — recovering from prior nadir; not at a level requiring transfusion or dose modification beyond what is already in place. Will continue to monitor.

4. Darbepoetin alfa (Aranesp) initiation — given the transfusion-dependent pattern of anemia while on a PARP inhibitor (two transfusions in the context of a 9-month olaparib course), and the intent to continue olaparib given the documented disease response in BRCA2-mutant mCRPC, an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent is being initiated today. This is being done with full understanding of the boxed warning context, acknowledging that Mr. Kowalski's anemia is related to non-myelosuppressive (bone marrow-suppressive via PARP inhibition rather than cytotoxic chemotherapy directly targeting marrow), and with a hemoglobin target of 10-12 g/dL to avoid transfusion dependence while not overshooting into the pro-thrombotic range. Darbepoetin alfa 500 mcg SQ will be administered every 3 weeks.

5. Renal function: Stable CKD Stage IIIa (eGFR ~52 mL/min), relevant to olaparib dosing. Olaparib at 150 mg BID is an appropriate dose for this level of renal function and in the context of myelosuppressive side effects. No further reduction is planned at this time; if the next CBC in 3 weeks shows continued marrow recovery and hemoglobin is stabilizing above 9-10 g/dL, we will reassess tolerability.

6. Goals of care and quality of life — Mr. Kowalski continues to express clear desire for active treatment while maintaining quality of life and time at home. His wife is supportive of this approach. The palliative care team remains involved. He understands that olaparib has produced meaningful disease response but that the anemia side effect has been a significant management challenge requiring close monitoring going forward.

---

PLAN

1. Olaparib (Lynparza): Resume today at 150 mg orally twice daily (further reduced from 200 mg BID given recurrent Grade 3 anemia). Hemoglobin today is 9.6 g/dL — above the 9 g/dL threshold — with reticulocyte recovery evident. Patient counseled on signs/symptoms of worsening anemia (dizziness, dyspnea at rest, chest pain) and instructed to call immediately or go to the nearest ED if these worsen acutely before next visit.

2. Darbepoetin alfa (Aranesp) 500 mcg SQ: Administered in infusion center today (first dose). Subsequent doses every 3 weeks. Hemoglobin target 10-12 g/dL. Patient counseled on the role of this medication in supporting red cell production in the context of olaparib-associated suppression. Risks discussed including hypertension (blood pressure monitored closely), and thromboembolic events (patient currently on no anticoagulation; DVT prophylaxis discussed, low-dose aspirin already not in use; will reassess if hemoglobin climbs beyond 12 g/dL, will hold darbepoetin at that point).

3. CBC with differential: Repeat in 3 weeks (at next visit, coinciding with next darbepoetin dose). Will assess hemoglobin trajectory, platelet recovery, and ANC. If hemoglobin is declining toward 8 g/dL or falling from today's level despite darbepoetin, will reassess olaparib continuation vs. permanent discontinuation.

4. Comprehensive metabolic panel: Every 4 weeks with each darbepoetin visit; monitor renal function, LFTs, electrolytes.

5. PSA: Repeat in 3-4 weeks. Current PSA of 4.4 ng/mL in the setting of olaparib hold is anticipated to stabilize or decline once drug is back on board. If PSA continues rising on resumed olaparib at 150 mg BID with no apparent dose-hold effect, will obtain confirmatory imaging and consider next-line options. Given PSA was 3.9 at 6-month imaging with radiographic partial response, a PSA below that mark or at least stable would be reassuring.

6. Leuprolide (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months: Continue; next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Testosterone confirmed castrate today.

7. Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SQ: Continue monthly (next dose in 3 weeks). Dental hygiene review performed; patient denies any tooth pain or jaw problems — no signs of osteonecrosis of the jaw. Calcium 500 mg and vitamin D 400 IU supplementation continued.

8. Folic acid supplementation: Continue for nutritional support given macrocytic marrow pattern.

9. Ondansetron (Zofran) 4-8 mg PRN oral: Continue for intermittent nausea.

10. Nutrition: Albumin mildly low at 3.4 g/dL; referred to registered dietitian for nutritional optimization. Encouraged high-protein diet. No signs of malnutrition syndrome at present.

11. Palliative care: Ongoing co-management. Patient and family continue to engage productively with palliative care team regarding symptom management, advance care planning, and psychosocial support.

12. Next office visit: In 3 weeks for CBC recheck, darbepoetin dose 2, and clinical reassessment. If doing well at that visit, will schedule restaging imaging (bone scan + CT chest/abdomen/pelvis) at the 9-10 month mark to formally reassess disease trajectory.

13. Laboratory follow-up: Iron studies to be repeated at 6-week mark if no response to darbepoetin to evaluate functional iron deficiency in the setting of erythropoietic stimulation.

Attending Oncologist: [Signature on file]
Dictated and reviewed by treating oncologist
Patient seen and examined personally; all findings discussed with patient and spouse in detail.
